segments.title: Bomb vessels at Copenhagen
segments.summary: A game battle prompts a concise explanation of why British bomb vessels mattered at Copenhagen in 1801.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes bomb vessels as ships used to bombard ports and cities after an enemy fleet has been dealt with. At Copenhagen, getting them into firing position was central to the British plan: once their mortars could threaten the city, the pressure on Denmark-Norway changed decisively. The moment connects naval control with coercive diplomacy rather than treating the fleet action as an end in itself.

evidence.note: Explains the historical role of bomb vessels and their purpose at Copenhagen in 1801.

segments.title: Could fleets fight in Arctic winter?
segments.summary: A viewer question distinguishes uncommon Arctic winter fleet actions from more frequent Baltic operations in wintry conditions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke says that full fleet actions in Arctic winter conditions were rare, while single-ship actions occurred more often. He adds that the answer depends on geography: fleets in the inner Baltic did operate in wintry conditions with some regularity. The distinction is a useful reminder that weather constraints change with the theatre rather than applying uniformly across naval warfare.
segments.question: Were naval battles fought in winter Arctic conditions?
segments.answerShort: Full fleet actions were rare in Arctic winter conditions, though single-ship actions occurred; wintry fleet operations were more regular in the inner Baltic.

evidence.note: Answers whether Arctic winter naval actions occurred and distinguishes the Baltic theatre.
